conference.name: 7th IEEE International Conference on Autonomic Computing and Self-Organizing Systems - ACSOS 2026
conference.programStatus: The conference data includes 27 accepted papers and the tentative program-at-a-glance timetable. Rooms and individual paper-to-session assignments are not available yet.
conference.description: The IEEE International Conference on Autonomic Computing and Self-Organizing Systems (ACSOS) is the leading forum to share the latest research results, ideas and experiences in autonomic computing, self-adaptation and self-organization. Now in its 7th edition, ACSOS was founded in 2020 as a merger of the IEEE International Conference on Autonomic Computing (ICAC) and the IEEE International Conference on Self-Adaptive and Self-Organizing Systems (SASO).

infoPages.title: Venue: University of Bologna, Cesena Campus
infoPages.body: The Cesena Campus is part of the University of Bologna (UniBo), the oldest continuously operating university in the Western world. Founded in 1088, UniBo now ranks among the top 200 universities globally and serves approximately 85,000 students across five campuses in Emilia-Romagna. The Cesena Campus was established in the 1980s as part of the university's territorial expansion strategy. Since then, it has evolved into a specialized hub for engineering, computer science, and technological disciplines, hosting around 5,000 students. Conference Rooms: Aula Magna The Aula Magna "Carmen Tura" (Room 3.4) is located on the first floor of the university building (level 3). Upon entering the building, you can find it to the left of the main entrance. Finally, to reach the campus, please take a look to the map below Location Via dell'Università, 50 47521 Cesena Italy Rooms 2.11

infoPages.title: Registration
infoPages.body: Registration for the 7th IEEE International Conference on Autonomic Computing and Self-Organizing Systems (ACSOS 2026) is open. Please read the registration information carefully before beginning the online registration. You can register for ACSOS 2026 via the following link: https://cvent.me/RyXPon All fees are quoted in US dollars ($USD) and include all taxes. Please email ieeecs-reg+ACSOS@computer.org if you need any registration assistance. For questions regarding author submissions, please contact Danilo Pianini at danilo.pianini@unibo.it and Ivana Dusparic at ivana.dusparic@tcd.ie . For questions regarding visas , please contact Mirko D'Angelo at

infoPages.title: Main Social Event
infoPages.body: The IEEE ACSOS 2026 conference reception will take place at Teatro Verdi, a historic theatre in the centre of Cesena that has been transformed into a distinctive venue for contemporary events. The theatre retains the character and elegance of its original architecture. Its combination of theatrical atmosphere, modern event facilities, and in-house catering will provide an informal and memorable setting for participants to meet, continue discussions, and connect with colleagues. Located in the heart of Cesena, Teatro Verdi also offers an opportunity to experience one of the city’s most characteristic venues during the conference. The Dinner venue is located ~25 minutes by walking, or ~10 - 15 minutes by bus or car.

socialEvents.title: Wine, Views, and Dinner on Romagna’s Balcony
socialEvents.body: Fee: €119 Capacity: minimum 30 participants, maximum 50 participants Includes: transportation, wine tasting, dinner Join us for a special ACSOS evening in Bertinoro , the renowned “Terrazza della Romagna” — Romagna’s balcony — combining local wine, traditional cuisine, panoramic views, and informal conversation with the conference community. The evening begins with a wine tasting at Tenuta La Viola :

socialEvents.title: ACSOS GP on the Riviera: Racing & Dinner
socialEvents.includes: transportation, karting option, Riccione walk option, dinner
socialEvents.body: Fee: €129 kart race + dinner / €99 dinner only Capacity: minimum 30 participants, maximum 50 participants Includes: transportation, karting option, Riccione walk option, dinner Karting capacity: minimum 14 drivers, maximum 32 drivers Join us for a special ACSOS evening on the Romagna Riviera , combining go-kart racing, seaside atmosphere, and an informal dinner with the conference community. The event begins at Riccione Circuit , a go-kart track located a short walk from the beach in Riccione:

socialEvents.title: Along Leonardo’s Canal: Cesenatico and Fish Dinner
socialEvents.includes: transportation, guided museum visit, guided city-centre walk, fish dinner
socialEvents.body: Fee: €129 Includes: transportation, guided museum visit, guided city-centre walk, fish dinner Restaurant: to be confirmed Join us for a special ACSOS evening in Cesenatico , combining maritime history, a guided walk through one of the most distinctive town centres on the Romagna coast, and a fish dinner with the conference community. The event begins with a guided visit to the Museo della Marineria , located along Cesenatico’s Porto Canale Leonardesco :

keynotes.speaker: Marco Dorigo
keynotes.title: Bridging Centralized and Decentralized Control in Robot Swarms through Self-Organizing Hierarchies
keynotes.abstract: Robot swarms promise scalable and resilient solutions for applications such as environmental monitoring, search and rescue, and logistics, yet their adoption remains limited by poor controllability. Fully self-organizing swarms achieve robustness and scalability through decentralized coordination and simple local interactions, but this comes at the cost of limited observability and difficulty in shaping global behavior. Conversely, centralized approaches offer ease of control but suffer from scalability issues and single points of failure. In this talk, I present the Self-Organizing Nervous System for Robot Swarms (SoNS), a middleware framework that enables robots to dynamically form adaptive hierarchical structures. SoNS bridges centralized and decentralized paradigms by supporting functionally centralized coordination of sensing, actuation, and decision-making, while preserving the scalability, flexibility, and fault tolerance of self-organizing systems.

keynotes.speaker: Ivona Brandic
keynotes.title: Bridging Classical and Quantum Computing: Lessons, Challenges, and Opportunities
keynotes.abstract: Quantum computing promises computational capabilities that extend beyond the representational and computational limits of classical systems. It is widely regarded as a candidate technology for addressing scalability and tractability challenges in domains such as molecular simulation, optimization, and artificial intelligence. However, classical high-performance computing (HPC) systems continue to play a crucial role in the quantum computing ecosystem. Classical computers are essential for tasks such as quantum device calibration, quantum state preparation, error mitigation, and data pre- and post-processing. In this talk, we critically examine the role of classical computing in conjunction with quantum systems. We revisit early misconceptions about quantum advantage, discuss key lessons learned from the first generation of hybrid quantum–classical systems, and highlight current research directions. Particular attention is given to state-of-the-art hybrid algorithms, including the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) and the Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQA), as well as emerging concepts for tighter integration between classical and quantum infrastructures. We conclude by exploring novel interaction paradigms, such as digital shadows and their practical implementations, which may shape the next generation of hybrid computing systems.

keynotes.speaker: Carlo Ghezzi
keynotes.title: Being a researcher — in turbulent times
keynotes.abstract: In 2020 I wrote a book titled “ Being a Researcher— An Informatics Perspective ”, in which I tried to share what I learned in my long journey through research, targeting especially young scientists and telling them what I wish I knew when I embarked on the journey. This talk reflects on what happened since. After less than a decade, the research landscape is undergoing a paradigm change, in the Kuhnian sense, mainly due to the advances in our own discipline, and in particular, the AI revolution. This has generated radical changes in the way research is performed, in all fields. But technological disruption is not only a methodological challenge; it is above all an ethical and institutional one. The way society and individuals are affected by our research, and the responsibilities we have as researchers have in fact scaled up to unprecedented levels. Academic institutions need to rethink their role in the new world we are building. The talk reflects on our role and responsibility as researchers and educators in the new world— and on whether our institutions are ready for it.
